title: Ask the Policy, Not the Internet
context: A design review has been called for the new policy-guidance assistant inside Expenses. The Product Director wants a standout AI experience for the launch narrative. Compliance wants grounded answers with traceability. Customer Success wants administrators to maintain policy content without engineering bottlenecks, and Security is wary of widening access to employee data. The architect is expected to turn the slogans into a safe design direction.

questions.prompt: The Product Director says: "Let the assistant answer any employee travel question using both company policy and general web knowledge so it feels more intelligent." How do you respond?

options.text: That would create the strongest user experience because employees could ask anything and still get a useful answer. If we blend policy text with broader web knowledge, the assistant will feel more capable and reduce the need for people to search elsewhere. It also gives Product a more ambitious AI narrative and postpones the governance questions until the assistant has already grown in scope.
options.explanation: The product ambition is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it accepts a broader answer scope before checking whether the feature is guidance grounded in company policy or a general-purpose assistant.

options.text: I would keep the assistant grounded in governed policy sources and make that boundary explicit in the product. The core requirement is trustworthy in-flow guidance, not broad internet advice. If we blur those together, we weaken traceability and make it harder for users to know what they can rely on.
options.explanation: This demonstrates translating the slogan into a requirement. The answer names the actual job of the feature and protects traceability by refusing to let general knowledge masquerade as policy.

options.text: We should avoid conversational AI here entirely because it is too risky. Employees can read PDFs or static help articles until the market proves that assistants are dependable enough for enterprise policy use.
options.explanation: The risk awareness is reasonable. This is failure mode B — correct instinct, wrong execution: it responds to the concern by rejecting the feature rather than framing a bounded, safer version of it.

questions.prompt: Compliance asks: "Can the assistant also tell managers whether a borderline claim should be approved?" What do you say?

options.text: Yes, that is the logical next step. If the assistant already knows the policy, it can recommend approval outcomes as well and save managers time on routine decisions. It also gives Product a more ambitious AI narrative and postpones the governance questions until the assistant has already grown in scope.
options.explanation: The desire to improve efficiency is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it collapses policy guidance and business authorisation into one feature without respecting the decision boundary.

options.text: I would keep guidance and authorisation separate. The assistant can explain policy and point to the relevant rule, but approval remains a business decision owned by the workflow and the accountable manager. That boundary matters for audit, accountability, and user trust.
options.explanation: This demonstrates separating guidance from business authorisation decisions. The answer protects ownership and audit by refusing to let an advisory tool become the approver by implication.

options.text: Let us park that for a later governance meeting because approval semantics get complicated quickly. Once Legal and Compliance have defined the full operating model for AI decision support, we can revisit the idea.
options.explanation: The caution is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the architect can already draw the key boundary in the room by keeping advisory guidance separate from authorisation.

questions.prompt: Customer Success says: "Admins should be able to type quick clarifications straight into the assistant so we do not slow them down with policy publishing steps." How do you respond?

options.text: We should allow that because speed matters more than ceremony. If an administrator knows the answer, the assistant should surface it immediately and we can tidy the content model later. It also gives Product a more ambitious AI narrative and postpones the governance questions until the assistant has already grown in scope.
options.explanation: The desire for responsiveness is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it accepts an ungoverned content path that weakens source control and traceability.

options.text: I would keep one governed policy source and make the assistant consume that, even if we improve the publishing flow for admins. Fast updates are valuable, but they should still land through a traceable content model so users can see what policy they are relying on and when it changed.
options.explanation: This demonstrates anchoring on ownership and audit. The answer protects the single governed source while still acknowledging the operational need for faster policy updates.

options.text: That sounds risky enough that we should leave policy maintenance with engineering for now. It is better to accept slower updates than to let administrators change the assistant's answers directly.
options.explanation: The concern about governance is reasonable. This is failure mode B — correct instinct, wrong execution: it protects control by moving ownership to the wrong team instead of improving the governed admin path.

questions.prompt: Security asks: "Can the assistant look at the employee's role, department, and recent claims so it can tailor answers automatically?" What is your response?

options.text: Yes, personalisation will make the assistant feel much smarter. Pulling in employee context lets it answer with fewer follow-up questions and creates a more modern experience for the launch.
options.explanation: The product value is understandable. This is failure mode A — over-agreement: it widens data access before proving that the guidance problem truly requires it.

options.text: I would start with policy-grounded guidance that uses the minimum context needed to answer safely. If tailored answers require employee attributes later, we should add them deliberately and keep the boundary explicit so policy retrieval does not become a broad reader of operational data by default.
options.explanation: This demonstrates naming the simpler option and applying ownership and audit criteria. The answer preserves least privilege and forces the room to justify any expansion of the assistant's data boundary.

options.text: We need a separate privacy review before we can say anything useful on this point. Until that happens, I would avoid taking a position on whether the assistant can consume employee context.
options.explanation: The instinct to involve privacy is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the architect can still state the default boundary now and then use privacy review to validate, not replace, that framing.

questions.prompt: The room asks you to close. Which summary is strongest?

options.text: We are treating the assistant as policy-grounded guidance, not general internet advice or an approval engine; we will keep a governed policy source with traceable updates; and we will start with the minimum data boundary needed for safe answers rather than broad personalisation. That gives Product, Compliance, and Security a shared contract for the next design step.
options.explanation: This demonstrates correct meeting closure. It restates the decision, the boundaries, and the next design consequence without reopening the underlying debate.

options.text: The topic is important enough that we should wait for a wider AI governance framework before setting any architectural boundaries. That will help us avoid designing the assistant twice.
options.explanation: The desire for alignment is understandable. This is failure mode C — unnecessary deferral: the room already has enough information to set the feature boundary even if wider AI governance will continue in parallel.

options.text: We should aim high and design for policy answers, approval guidance, personalised context, and broad content flexibility together so the assistant can grow into a strategic platform component. That keeps our options open.
options.explanation: The ambition is understandable. This is failure mode D — scope explosion: it turns a bounded guidance feature into a much larger AI platform commitment before the current requirement has been stabilised.
